retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Connective Tissue -- Structure and Function -- Connective Tissue Proper
retrieved_docs.content: Connective tissue proper includes both loose (or areolar) connective tissue and dense connective tissue. Loose connective tissue previously included areolar, reticular, and adipose tissues, although this system has been revised only to include areolar tissue. Loose connective tissue generally holds organs, anatomic structures, and tissues in place. The extracellular matrix is the most significant feature of loose connective tissue with large spaces between fibers. Dense connective tissue proper is composed of a higher density of fibers, which may be regular (with parallel fibers such as that of tendons and ligaments) or irregular (with multidirectional fibers such as that of the pericardium), or elastic (with significant embedded elastin such as that of arteries).

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Connective Tissue -- Structure and Function -- Connective Tissue Proper
retrieved_docs.content: The extracellular matrix of both loose and dense connective tissue proper is composed of amorphous ground substance, extracellular fluid, and collagen and elastic fibers. The amorphous ground substance is a gelatinous material surrounding the fibers and permitting extracellular fluid to diffuse through. The collagen and elastic fibers of connective tissue proper are histologically distinguishable as three fiber types: collagen, reticular, and elastic fibers.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Connective Tissue -- Structure and Function -- Connective Tissue Proper
retrieved_docs.content: Collagen fibers form approximately 20-25% of the protein content of humans. They are nonelastic and have variable bundle thicknesses. They are made up of closely packed thin collagen fibrils that run a wavy course in tissues. These parallel fibrils form bundles with flexible proteoglycans to offer an essential mechanical property. They offer flexible but powerful resistance to pulling force. Specifically, collagen runs in a parallel course in loose connective tissue and then joins to form a larger bundle. They split from each other and join back together at varying locations, creating a three-dimensional meshwork. Dense connective tissue, such as ligaments and tendons, is composed mainly of densely packed collagen fibers. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Connective Tissue -- Structure and Function -- Connective Tissue Proper
retrieved_docs.content: Reticular fibers are generally recognized as one type of collagen fiber. They are also called argyrophilic fibers, which have a restricted abundance in the human body. They are primarily present in basement epithelial tissue, adipose cells, Schwann and muscle cells, lymphoid tissue, and endothelium of hepatic sinusoids. Under microscopy, these reticular fibers are fine, dark fibrils that are continuous with the college fibers described above. The arrangement of these fibers forms a network that underlies the basal lamina layer. A firm attachment of these fibers to the basal lamina indicates that these fibers, along with the collagen fibers, create a functional and structural unit that supports tissues. The loose arrangement of these fibers also provides space for molecular movement within the extracellular fluid. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Connective Tissue -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Connective tissue is one of the basic tissue types of the body. As its name implies, "connective tissue" refers to several body tissues that connect, support, and help bind other tissues. While the various connective tissues of the body are diverse, they share numerous structural and functional features that explain why they are subsumed into a single tissue category. Connective tissue can be broken down into two primary categories: connective tissue proper and specialized connective tissue. Connective tissue proper is further subdivided into loose and dense connective tissues. Specialized connective tissues are diverse as they comprise varying specialized cells and ground substances. Specialized connective tissues include adipose, cartilage, bone, blood, and reticular tissues. See Image. Connective Tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Connective Tissue -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Fibers are another main component of connective tissue found in the extracellular matrix. Three basic fiber types are present in all connective tissue. The amount of each type generally reflects the function and classification of the particular tissue. The 3 fiber types are: Collagenous fibers: large, strong fibers, mostly commonly Type I collagen, that provide high tensile strength to the extracellular matrix, found in dense and loose connective tissue. [1] Reticular fibers: delicate, thin fibers composed of Type III collagen that cross-link to form a supporting meshwork in the reticular lamina of the basement membrane found in soft tissues such as the liver, bone marrow, spleen, and lymph nodes. [4] Elastic fibers: thin, branching fibers made of elastin that provide stretch and recoil to the extracellular matrix, found in tissues such as the aorta, lung, skin, and vocal cords. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Brown Syndrome -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Tendon Abnormalities: The superior oblique tendon may be congenitally short, tight, or inelastic. Histologically, this could manifest as an increased density of collagen fibers, decreased spacing between fibers, or abnormalities in the composition of the extracellular matrix. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Lichen Planopilaris -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: A band-like mononuclear infiltrate obscuring the interface between the follicular epithelium and the dermis is observed in lichen planopilaris. The epithelial-stromal junction may have prominent vacuoles and dyskeratosis with individually necrotic, polygonal basal keratinocytes. Inflammation affects the upper portion of the follicle most severely, but this may extend down the length of the follicle. Perifollicular fibrosis and chronic inflammation are observed in later stages. Ultimately, follicles are replaced by columns of sclerotic collagen called follicular scars. Elastic tissue stains can be used to highlight these scars. Grouped globular immunofluorescence, typically IgM, especially when found adjacent to the follicular epithelium, is the characteristic pattern observed in lichen planopilaris. This pattern is in contrast to chronic cutaneous lupus erythematosus, where linear deposits of immunoreactants are typical. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Connective Tissue -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Connective tissue is an umbrella that encompasses a variety of tissue types, including loose and dense connective tissue, adipose, cartilage, bone, and blood. Although connective tissue is diverse, all connective tissue consists of 3 main components: Ground substance Fibers Cells

retrieved_docs.title: Necrobiosis Lipoidica -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Histopathologically, biopsy specimens from the palpable inflammatory border reveal scattered palisaded and interstitial granulomatous dermatitis, with layered tiers of granulomatous inflammation parallel to the skin surface, involving the entire dermis and extending into the subcutaneous fat septae. The granulomas comprise histiocytes, lymphocytes, plasma cells, and eosinophils and are organized around patches of collagen degeneration. Decreased or loss of nerve cells is common. The epidermis is normal or atrophic. Focal loss of elastic tissue can be observed in areas of connective tissue sclerosis. Unlike granuloma annulare, there is no significant mucin deposition in the center of the palisading granulomas (see Images. Low-Power Histological Findings of Necrobiosis Lipoidica; High-Power Pathological Findings of Necrobiosis Lipoidica: Skin Biopsy; and Pathological Findings of Necrobiosis Lipoidica: Skin Biopsy). [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Connective Tissue -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Connective tissue contributes to numerous body functions, including supporting organs and cells, transporting nutrients and wastes, defending against pathogens, storing fat, and repairing damaged tissues (see Image. Transverse Section of the Ureter). Connective tissue is composed primarily of an extracellular matrix and a limited number of cells. Most connective tissues are composed of ground substances, fibers, and cells, although blood and lymph are specialized fluid connective tissues without fiber.

retrieved_docs.title: Dermatofibrosarcoma Protuberans -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The spindle-shaped tumor cells of DFSP are arranged in a storiform or woven pattern, parallel to the epidermal surface, and have little pleomorphism and scant cytoplasm (see Image. Hematoxylin and Eosin Stain Dermatofibrosarcoma Protuberans). The cells are surrounded by collagenous stroma, sometimes associated with hyaline or myxoid changes. The characteristic honeycomb appearance results from irregular tentacle-like projections infiltrating the underlying subcutaneous tissue, traversing the septa and fat, leading to fat entrapment. DFSP typically extends into subcutaneous fat but seldom involves fascia, muscle, or bone unless recurrent or long-standing. Necrosis is uncommon. While mitoses are present, no significant mitotic activity is present, and atypical mitoses are rare. A mitotic count that reaches 10 mitoses per 10 high power field (HPF) and tumor size correlates with metastatic spread. About one-fifth of all DFSPs undergo fibrosarcomatous transformation, which appears as an expansive tumor with fascicular and herringbone patterns and atypical cytological features. [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Connective Tissue -- Structure and Function -- Connective Tissue Proper
retrieved_docs.content: Elastic fibers have the characteristic property of elastic recoil; each fiber can be stretched to approximately 150% of its resting length. These fibers are composed of elastin surrounded by fibrillin. Typically, in loose connective tissue, elastin is a loose network. Their organization and distribution depend on the type of tissue. Concentric elastin fibers are present in the vascular wall to help maintain uniform blood pressure. Fibers are also present in distensible and contractible organs such as the lungs and urinary bladder. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Connective Tissue -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: Collagen and elastin compose the dry mass of tendon connective tissue. [8] Tendons are connective tissue structures comprised of a hierarchical arrangement of collagen molecules that arrange into collagen fibrils and then collagen fibers, which can be grouped into primary, secondary, and tertiary bundles. [9] The tendon is surrounded by the epitenon, a fine connective tissue sheath. The complex multidimensional arrangement of the collagen fibers of tendons makes its function possible during the longitudinal, rotational, and transverse forces exerted upon tendons. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Dermis -- Microscopy, Light
retrieved_docs.content: Light microscopic analysis of H&E-stained samples delineates the epidermis, dermis, and subcutaneous adipose. The epidermis is easily visualized due to the presence of basophilic keratinocytes. Scanning across the tissue sample, one can appreciate the alternating dermal papillae and rete ridges. Dermal papillae are the protrusions of dermal connective tissue into the epidermal layer. Rete ridges are the extensions of the epidermis into the dermal layer. This undulating pattern is more apparent in the thick skin of the hands and palms. There is no clear line of distinction between the papillary and reticular dermis. Collagen patterns are mostly horizontal throughout. The superficial papillary dermis possesses thinner elastic fibers compared to the thicker elastic fibers of the reticular dermis. The papillary dermis is composed of loose connective tissue (LCT) and is highly vascular. The reticular dermis shows thick collagen bundles and forms the bulk of the dermal layer.

retrieved_docs.title: Retroperitoneal Fibrosis -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Fibroblastic proliferation with densely hyalinized collagen is arranged in a woven or spiral (storiform) pattern, accompanied by varying degrees of chronic lymphocytic and plasma cell infiltrates, which may be IgG4-positive. [1] The resulting fibrous tissue comprises an extracellular matrix of type-1 collagen fibers organized in thick, irregular bundles, often encircling smaller vessels. Vasculitis of small retroperitoneal vessels is observed in about 50% of cases. This is also described as a fibroproliferative process infiltrated by eosinophils and occasionally IgG4-positive plasma cells.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2564
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of bone and connective tissue is mapped to ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي, indicating that this malignant condition involves the upper jaw and reflects a neoplastic process affecting both bone and connective tissue structures in that region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2234
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of synovium, tendon, and bursa—conditions affecting the connective tissues that line joints, support tendons, and cushion bursae—are related to muscle disorders (اضطراب العضلات), highlighting an interconnection between joint‑associated soft‑tissue pathologies and muscular dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 362
retrieved_docs.content: Other bone disease and musculoskeletal deformities are associated with bone resorption, indicating that these conditions often involve increased breakdown of bone tissue, which contributes to skeletal weakening and structural abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2187
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of bone and connective tissue is a malignant condition that involves the skeletal system and its supporting connective tissues. It has a broader relationship with ورم العظام, indicating that bone tumors (ورم العظام) are encompassed within this wider category of cancer. Recognizing that ورم العظام represents a specific manifestation of cancer of bone and connective tissue aids clinicians in accurate diagnosis, appropriate classification, and targeted therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 143
retrieved_docs.content: Arthropathy associated with an endocrine AND/OR metabolic disorder is classified as other non‑traumatic joint disorders and its primary finding site is the joints (klouby). Other non‑traumatic joint disorders are linked to the concept of اعتلال مفصلي (joint disease). In the context of surgical procedures, Division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage is a subcategory (has parent) of Incision and excision of joint structures and it classifies the anatomical category “division of joint capsule; ligament or cartilage.” Incision and excision of joint structures also has a relationship with the joints (klouby). This integrated knowledge connects endocrine‑related arthropathy to non‑traumatic joint pathology, emphasizes its joint involvement, and outlines the hierarchical relationship between joint‑focused surgical interventions and the specific joint structures they address.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 179
retrieved_docs.content: acceleration (تَسَرُّعُ القَلْبِ الأُذَينِيّ المنتبذ), conduction disorders, and connective‑tissue disorder (اضطراب الأنسجة الضامة) are organized hierarchically; “كلاء الكليون السفلي” is linked to acute and unspecified renal failure. Additionally, other circulatory disease is classified as a subtype of connective‑tissue disorder (اضطراب الأنسجة الضامة) and is clinically associated with systemic lupus erythematosus (الذئبة الحمراء المجموعية). Collectively, these relationships demonstrate how abnormalities in cardiac electrical conduction, specific tachyarrhythmias, structural heart disease, procedural interventions, drug contraindications, and connective‑tissue disorders intersect in the assessment, classification, and management of patients with cardiac rhythm disorders, heart failure, and related systemic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 1174
retrieved_docs.content: shared therapeutic goals for musculoskeletal and connective‑tissue conditions. This integrated framework supports comprehensive patient care by aligning specific modalities with appropriate clinical indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1851
retrieved_docs.content: The peritoneum serves as the anatomical finding site for فتق البطن (abdominal hernia) and is also the location implicated in اضطراب صِفَاقِيّ (serosal disorder), indicating that peritoneal involvement is central to both the presentation of abdominal hernias and the development of serosal disturbances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2734
retrieved_docs.content: Kolagen serves as the parent classification for connective tissue proteins, indicating that connective tissue proteins are members of the kolagen family.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1148
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic lupus erythematosus (الذئبة الحمراء المجموعية) is clinically associated with other circulatory disease, and this category of circulatory disease is classified as a subtype of connective tissue disorder (اضطراب الأنسجة الضامة). This relationship underscores that cardiovascular manifestations observed in lupus are part of the broader spectrum of connective tissue diseases, highlighting the importance of integrated cardiovascular evaluation and management in patients with systemic lupus erythematosus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1238
retrieved_docs.content: choroid scar all represent reparative or fibrotic sequelae that localize to the choroidal layer after injury or inflammation. Degenerative changes are represented by partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, senile choroidal atrophy (الضمور الشيخوخي بالمشيمية), and secondary spread of choroidal atrophy (انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية), reflecting age‑related or progressive loss of choroidal structure and function. Traumatic disruptions of the choroid include choroidal rupture (التمزق المشيمي) and choroidal inversion (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة), while fluid‑related detachments are described as serous choroidal detachment (انفصال المشيمية المصلية) and general choroidal detachment of the eye (انفصال مشيمية العين). Congenital and hereditary abnormalities are captured by the terms congenital malformation of the choroid (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية), partial perimacular choroidal calcification (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي), and hereditary choroidal calcification (حَثَلُ

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 186
retrieved_docs.content: described using the full range of qualifiers (abnormality, anatomy, histology, cytology, innervation, blood supply, growth and development, transplantation, injury, and drug effects), demonstrating that morphological, physiological, pathological, and therapeutic dimensions are consistently integrated for patient assessment, diagnosis, and management across both musculoskeletal and vascular systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 181
retrieved_docs.content: mononeuritis, and multifocal mononeuritis exemplify inflammatory involvement of peripheral nerves. Diabetic, idiopathic progressive, malignancy‑associated, collagen‑vascular disease, and severe polyneuropathies are all classified as peripheral neuropathies. “اعتلال مرتكز العظم” (bone‑centered neuropathy) is linked to tendon‑sheath inflammation, indicating an interaction between osseous neural changes and tenosynovial inflammation. Collectively, the hierarchy and relational network portray “Other disorders of nervous system” as a comprehensive diagnostic framework that integrates neuropathic pain, focal nerve lesions, muscle‑nerve disorders, hereditary and idiopathic neuropathies, toxic/inflammatory/vascular neuropathies, drug‑induced and metabolic polyneuropathies, chronic demyelinating diseases, genetic neuropathies, and distinct syndromes. This unified structure supports coding, diagnosis, and patient‑centered management of a broad array of neurological conditions, emphasizing the

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2634
retrieved_docs.content: (تنكس حصوي) and choroidal atrophy (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة), while localized calcification around the optic disc (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي) represents a specific form of mineral deposition. Finally, choroidal scarring (ندبة مشيمية شبكية) denotes fibrotic remodeling after injury or inflammation. Collectively, these entities underscore the choroidea’s central role in ocular disease, highlighting the need for careful clinical assessment and targeted management of choroidal involvement across diverse pathological contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1907
retrieved_docs.content: الالْتِهاب (inflammation) is associated with other bone disease and musculoskeletal deformities, indicating that inflammatory processes can contribute to the development or worsening of skeletal disorders and structural abnormalities of the musculoskeletal system. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as addressing underlying inflammation may help prevent or mitigate bone pathology and deformities, thereby improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 847
retrieved_docs.content: Abdomen destructive procedure is the broader category that includes the specific operative technique of excision; lysis peritoneal adhesions. This procedure is performed indirectly on the peritoneum, targeting adhesions within the serosal membrane of the abdominal cavity. The peritoneum itself serves as the anatomical site where clinical findings such as فتق البطن (abdominal hernia) are identified, and it is also a location susceptible to اِضطِراب صِفَاقِيّ (membranous or serosal disorder). Recognizing that excision; lysis peritoneal adhesions acts on the peritoneum to resolve adhesions, while the peritoneum may also be involved in hernias and membranous disorders, provides clinicians with a comprehensive view of how therapeutic interventions, diagnostic findings, and pathological conditions are interconnected in abdominal care.
